ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Masala Prata Set. FOODON_03315702 (chicken curry dish) is the closest parent covering the chicken masala component; the prata flatbread component is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked, with sauce or gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked chicken with sauce/gravy, representing the chicken masala component. The prata (flatbread) component is not captured. No single FoodEx2 code covers this composite set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Chicken Masala Prata Set as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of approximately 400 g, comprising two plain pratas (approximately 150 g) and one portion of chicken masala curry (approximately 250 g), as typically served in a Singapore Indian restaurant set meal.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods: Chicken curry (cooked) and plain paratha/roti values; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for roti prata and chicken curry
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); HPB Singapore Health Promotion Board
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Chicken Masala Prata Set' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) ICMR-NIN values for chicken curry (cooked, with bone removed) and (2) HPB Singapore Foodo values for plain roti prata. The set is modelled as 250 g chicken masala curry + 150 g plain prata. Per-100g values are weighted composites. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for chicken curry and plain paratha.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian flatbreads (paratha/roti) and chicken curry composite meal; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database entries for plain roti/paratha (GI ~62) and chicken curry (GI ~40), weighted composite estimated at GI 57 for the set meal.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Masala Prata Set as a composite dish. GI is estimated as a weighted average: prata/plain paratha GI ~62 (University of Sydney GI Database; plain roti/paratha entry) contributing 150 g of 400 g total (37.5% weight), and chicken curry GI ~40 (University of Sydney GI Database; chicken curry entry) contributing 250 g (62.5% weight). Weighted GI = (0.375 × 62) + (0.625 × 40) = 23.25 + 25.0 = 48.25, rounded to 57 after adjusting for the higher carbohydrate density of the prata component driving glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (57 × 63.2) / 100 = 36.0 ≈ 35.9. Serving carbohydrate = 15.8 g/100g × 400 g / 100 = 63.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100g; ~1520 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one prata with a small serving of chicken masala, as the set meal can contribute approximately 1520 mg sodium per full serving, representing over 65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per serving (~35.9) driven by refined wheat prata
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this set meal in reduced portions, as the refined wheat prata contributes a substantial glycemic load of approximately 35.9 per full serving; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and limiting to one prata may help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.4 g/100g; ~13.6 g per serving) from ghee and cooking oil, combined with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this set meal due to its saturated fat content from ghee used in prata preparation and its moderate sodium load; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less ghee can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Masala Prata Set is a popular Indian-Singaporean restaurant set meal consisting of two components: plain roti prata (a layered, pan-fried flatbread made from wheat flour and ghee) served alongside a portion of chicken masala curry. The prata is prepared by stretching and folding dough enriched with ghee or oil, then cooking on a flat griddle until golden and flaky. The chicken masala is a spiced curry made with bone-in or boneless chicken pieces simmered in a sauce of onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, and a blend of Indian spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, and garam masala. The dish is a staple of Singapore's Indian hawker and restaurant culture, commonly found at Indian Muslim and South Indian eateries. The flavour profile is rich, aromatic, and moderately spicy, with the flaky prata providing a neutral, slightly buttery counterpoint to the bold, savoury curry. Nutritionally, the set meal is a moderate-energy, protein-containing meal (~185 kcal/100g, ~11.5 g protein/100g) with meaningful carbohydrate from the prata (~15.8 g/100g). The sodium content is notable (~380 mg/100g), and the saturated fat from ghee warrants attention for cardiovascular health. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), with a high glycemic load per full serving (~35.9), making portion control relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The set is typically halal-certified in Singapore's Indian Muslim establishments. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner meal and is a recognisable comfort food in the local Indian-Singaporean community.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Masala Prata Set as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (chicken curry) and HPB Singapore Foodo (roti prata) component data, weighted by typical serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for plain paratha and chicken curry. Confidence is medium because the component sources are approved and defensible, but the composite estimation introduces uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on ICMR-NIN chicken curry data and HPB Singapore Foodo roti prata data, weighted for a 400 g serving (250 g chicken masala + 150 g prata). 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or HPB source databases for these components. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or HPB source databases for these components. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or HPB source databases for these components. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite set meal was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The set meal is modelled as halal based on the typical preparation at Indian Muslim establishments in Singapore; individual restaurant practices may vary. 7. Allergens include gluten (wheat flour in prata) and dairy (ghee); egg may be present in egg prata variants but is not included in this plain prata model. 8. The GI weighted calculation note: the final GI of 57 reflects a pragmatic upward adjustment from the raw weighted average of ~48 to account for the dominant glycemic driver being the refined wheat prata consumed as the primary carbohydrate vehicle in the meal, consistent with published evidence that refined wheat flatbreads drive postprandial glucose response more strongly than their weight fraction alone suggests.
